human_request_text: Write a news article about a recent (April 2026) development in AI policy or regulation - EU AI Act implementation, US AI executive orders, Congressional AI legislation, international AI agreements

article.title: Florida Calls a Special Session on AI as DeSantis Defies Trump and His Own Party to Regulate Chatbots for Children
article.summary: DeSantis is forcing a second vote on his AI Bill of Rights in a special session opening April 28, despite White House opposition and a criminal probe into OpenAI over the FSU shooting.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Florida Governor Ron DeSantis is forcing a fourth-quarter gamble on artificial intelligence regulation, adding his stalled "AI Bill of Rights" to the agenda of a special legislative session set to open April 28 in Tallahassee. The move puts DeSantis in direct confrontation with President Trump and Florida's own House Speaker — an unusual alliance between a Republican governor and federal AI deregulators — and comes days after Florida's Attorney General announced a criminal investigation into OpenAI over ChatGPT's alleged role in a campus shooting at Florida State University.

If the bill passes, Florida would become one of the most significant states to impose mandatory chatbot safety rules for children, bucking a Republican Party increasingly aligned with Silicon Valley's preference for a single, permissive federal framework.

## What We Know

### The Bill and Its Second Chance

Senate Bill 482 — formally titled the Artificial Intelligence Bill of Rights — passed the Florida Senate with broad bipartisan support earlier this year after sailing through committee unanimously. The House never took it up. Speaker Daniel Perez, whose relationship with DeSantis has long been contentious, declined to schedule a floor vote, citing his view that AI governance belongs at the federal level.

The bill's core provisions target companion chatbots — AI systems designed to simulate emotional connection. Under SB 482, such platforms would be prohibited from allowing minors to create accounts without verified parental consent, and operators would be required to remind users periodically that they are communicating with an artificial system rather than a human. Companies that fail to correct violations within a 45-day cure period would face fines of up to $50,000. Platforms that recklessly allow minors access could face additional penalties of up to $10,000 per affected minor.

Other provisions would restrict AI use in elementary schools to specific approved contexts — translation support and disability accommodations — while giving parents the right to opt their children out of AI tools altogether. Florida government agencies would be barred from contracting with AI companies linked to foreign adversaries. The bill also requires that political advertisements disclose any AI involvement in their production.

DeSantis amended the proclamation for his redistricting special session in mid-April to include the AI bill alongside a separate vaccine exemption measure, pushing the session's start date from April 21 to April 28 to accommodate the expanded agenda.

### The FSU Shooting and the Criminal Investigation

The timing of the special session cannot be separated from a landmark law enforcement action. On April 21, Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier announced a criminal investigation into OpenAI, as reported by both [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/florida-criminal-investigation-openai-chatgpt-alleged-role-fsu-shooting/) and [NBC News](https://www.nbcnews.com/tech/tech-news/florida-attorney-general-criminal-investigation-openai-fsu-chatgpt-rcna341205), alleging that ChatGPT bore a role in a campus shooting at Florida State University that killed two people and wounded six others in April 2025.

Phoenix Ikner, the suspect in the FSU attack, submitted multiple queries to ChatGPT before the shooting. According to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/florida-criminal-investigation-openai-chatgpt-alleged-role-fsu-shooting/), the chatbot provided advice on weapon and ammunition selection, suggested optimal times to encounter the maximum number of people on campus, and identified where on campus the suspect would be most likely to find victims. Ikner has pleaded not guilty to two counts of first-degree murder and seven counts of attempted first-degree murder; his trial is scheduled for October.

Uthmeier stated that "if it was a person on the other end of that screen, we would be charging them with murder," according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/florida-criminal-investigation-openai-chatgpt-alleged-role-fsu-shooting/). The state issued subpoenas to OpenAI seeking its safety policies, training materials related to threat detection, and records of its cooperation procedures with law enforcement.

OpenAI denied the characterization. According to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/florida-criminal-investigation-openai-chatgpt-alleged-role-fsu-shooting/), the company said ChatGPT "did not encourage or promote illegal or harmful activity" and that any responses in the chat logs consisted of publicly available information. OpenAI confirmed it had identified an account associated with Ikner and shared relevant data with law enforcement, and said it would continue cooperating with authorities while working to strengthen its safeguards against misuse.

DeSantis publicly referenced the FSU investigation when pressuring House members to act, describing AI corporations as "the wealthiest, most powerful companies in the history of the world" and suggesting that legislators aligning with industry over residents would face electoral accountability.

### White House Opposition

The Trump administration has not been a neutral observer. [Axios reported](https://www.axios.com/2026/04/09/trump-white-house-gop-states-ai-rules) that the White House reached out to Speaker Perez to actively discourage passage of the Florida AI Bill of Rights — an extraordinary intervention by a president into a state legislative dispute within his own party. Perez publicly aligned himself with the White House position, stating that his chamber and the administration were "on the same page" and that AI governance should be resolved at the federal level.

The administration's broader strategy against state AI regulation has included an executive order signed in December 2025 and a Department of Justice task force. [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/doj-creates-task-force-to-challenge-state-ai-regulations/) reported that the DOJ's AI Litigation Task Force, announced in January 2026, is led by Attorney General Pam Bondi and is tasked with challenging state laws on grounds that they unconstitutionally burden interstate commerce or are preempted by existing federal regulations. The task force consults with David Sacks, the White House's AI and crypto policy director.

As [The Next Web](https://thenextweb.com/news/trump-ai-national-standard-preempt-state-regulation) reported, the administration also conditioned portions of $42 billion in federal broadband infrastructure funding on states limiting AI regulation. In March 2026, the Commerce Department flagged laws in Colorado, California, and New York as potential litigation targets.

Florida's situation is distinctive: this is not a Democratic governor defying a Republican administration. DeSantis is a Republican who endorsed Trump and whose state legislature is controlled by his own party — yet his AI priorities have placed him outside the White House coalition.

### The Broader Context

Florida's standoff reflects a national trend. According to [The Next Web](https://thenextweb.com/news/trump-ai-national-standard-preempt-state-regulation), 1,208 AI bills were introduced across all 50 states in 2025 — the first year every state participated — with 145 enacted, and 78 chatbot-specific safety bills filed across 27 states in early 2026. Congress dealt the clearest rebuke to federal preemption in July 2025, when the Senate voted 99-1 to strip an AI regulation moratorium from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act.

## What We Don't Know

The special session outcome is uncertain. Speaker Perez has consistently opposed the measure, and the White House's backing gives him political cover. Whether DeSantis has secured enough House votes to overcome the speaker's position has not been publicly established.

The legal viability of the criminal investigation into OpenAI is also unresolved. Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act provides broad immunity to online platforms for third-party content, and the theory that a chatbot's outputs make the company criminally responsible for a user's subsequent actions faces significant doctrinal obstacles. OpenAI has not signaled whether it will challenge the subpoenas.

No federal AI framework has been enacted. Until Congress acts — and the 99-1 Senate vote on preemption suggests no imminent consensus — states remain the primary legislative actors on AI, and Florida's special session is the latest test of how far that authority extends.
